Example Phrase: I lost my phone and changed my number
Why It Matters: Criminals often use phone loss or number changes as part of SIM swap account takeover.
Use With Context: Combine with new payee, unusual transfers, profile changes, or 2FA issues. Verify customer using known contact information.
Primary Sources: FBI Cybercrime; FTC Identity Theft; FinCEN SAR Data

Example Phrase: My phone suddenly had no service
Why It Matters: Unexpected loss of service can indicate the phone number was moved to another SIM or carrier account.
Use With Context: Use with login anomalies, customer confusion, or attempted password resets. Contact the carrier if appropriate.
Primary Sources: FBI Cybercrime; FTC Identity Theft; FinCEN SAR Data

Example Phrase: The carrier said my SIM was changed
Why It Matters: A SIM change can let a criminal intercept banking authentication codes.
Use With Context: Use with recent profile changes, password resets, or new device activity. Escalate if money movement follows.
Primary Sources: FBI Cybercrime; FTC Identity Theft; FinCEN SAR Data

Example Phrase: My phone number was ported without permission
Why It Matters: Unauthorized number porting is a strong account takeover signal.
Use With Context: Use with failed 2FA, new payees, or digital banking changes. Verify identity out of band.
Primary Sources: FBI Cybercrime; FTC Identity Theft; FinCEN SAR Data

Example Phrase: I received a SIM change notification
Why It Matters: A carrier SIM change notification can indicate account compromise or attempted compromise.
Use With Context: Use with suspicious login timing, profile updates, or customer reports of missing texts.
Primary Sources: FBI Cybercrime; FTC Identity Theft; FinCEN SAR Data

Example Phrase: I got a two factor code I did not request
Why It Matters: Unrequested authentication codes can mean someone is attempting to access the customer's account.
Use With Context: Use with login attempts, credential resets, or new device alerts. Recommend credential reset if needed.
Primary Sources: FBI Cybercrime; FTC Identity Theft; FinCEN SAR Data

Example Phrase: Authentication codes stopped coming to my phone
Why It Matters: Missing security codes after phone issues may mean codes are being intercepted elsewhere.
Use With Context: Use with recent carrier changes or failed login attempts. Confirm phone ownership before changes.
Primary Sources: FBI Cybercrime; FTC Identity Theft; FinCEN SAR Data

Example Phrase: A new device logged in after my phone changed
Why It Matters: New device access after phone changes is a strong digital compromise pattern.
Use With Context: Use with new payees, outgoing transfers, or profile edits. Consider temporary holds or step-up verification.
Primary Sources: FBI Cybercrime; FTC Identity Theft; FinCEN SAR Data

Example Phrase: My carrier account password was changed
Why It Matters: Carrier account takeover can precede banking account takeover.
Use With Context: Use with phone number changes, authentication failures, and attempted online banking access.
Primary Sources: FBI Cybercrime; FTC Identity Theft; FinCEN SAR Data

Example Phrase: Someone tried to move my phone number
Why It Matters: Attempted number movement can indicate a SIM swap or port-out fraud attempt.
Use With Context: Use with any money movement request. Verify identity and document carrier details.
Primary Sources: FBI Cybercrime; FTC Identity Theft; FinCEN SAR Data

Example Phrase: My doctor said I need to pay for treatment immediately
Why It Matters: Healthcare urgency can pressure customers into immediate payments before verification.
Use With Context: Combine with wire, ACH, gift card, or crypto requests. Verify with provider using a known phone number.
Primary Sources: FTC Healthcare Scams; FBI Medicare Fraud; AARP Healthcare Fraud

Example Phrase: Hospital billing called and demanded payment today
Why It Matters: Urgent hospital billing calls can be impersonation attempts, especially when payment method is unusual.
Use With Context: Use with new payee or urgency language. Ask customer to call the hospital number on an official statement.
Primary Sources: FTC Healthcare Scams; FBI Medicare Fraud; AARP Healthcare Fraud

Example Phrase: The insurance company said coverage will be cancelled today
Why It Matters: Threatening loss of coverage can create pressure for fast payment or credential sharing.
Use With Context: Use with caller ID spoofing, payment urgency, or requests for banking credentials.
Primary Sources: FTC Healthcare Scams; FBI Medicare Fraud; AARP Healthcare Fraud

Example Phrase: Medicare called asking for my bank account
Why It Matters: Medicare impersonation commonly targets older customers for bank details or identity information.
Use With Context: Use with senior clients, SSN requests, or account verification language. Verify through official Medicare channels.
Primary Sources: FTC Healthcare Scams; FBI Medicare Fraud; AARP Healthcare Fraud

Example Phrase: Medicaid needs a payment before benefits continue
Why It Matters: Benefit-continuation threats can be used to steal payments or personal information.
Use With Context: Use with urgency, prepaid cards, wire transfers, or requests for account information.
Primary Sources: FTC Healthcare Scams; FBI Medicare Fraud; AARP Healthcare Fraud

Example Phrase: The pharmacy said I must pay the copay by wire
Why It Matters: Wire payment for a pharmacy copay is unusual and may indicate impersonation.
Use With Context: Use with new payee, unusual payment method, or caller pressure. Verify with the pharmacy directly.
Primary Sources: FTC Healthcare Scams; FBI Medicare Fraud; AARP Healthcare Fraud

Example Phrase: My medical test results require a payment first
Why It Matters: Scammers may use sensitive medical topics to pressure victims into fast payments.
Use With Context: Use with links, new portals, or requests for card/bank information. Confirm through the provider portal or known phone number.
Primary Sources: FTC Healthcare Scams; FBI Medicare Fraud; AARP Healthcare Fraud

Example Phrase: The telehealth provider asked for gift cards
Why It Matters: Gift card payment is inconsistent with legitimate medical billing.
Use With Context: Use as a high-risk signal when paired with urgent medical language. Warn customer before funds leave.
Primary Sources: FTC Healthcare Scams; FBI Medicare Fraud; AARP Healthcare Fraud

Example Phrase: Treatment will be delayed if I do not send money
Why It Matters: Delay threats create emotional pressure and can bypass normal verification habits.
Use With Context: Use with urgent transfer requests or third-party payment instructions. Verify provider independently.
Primary Sources: FTC Healthcare Scams; FBI Medicare Fraud; AARP Healthcare Fraud

Example Phrase: A health app asked me to verify my bank information
Why It Matters: Fake health apps may collect bank credentials or card information under verification language.
Use With Context: Use with app download, link click, or login changes. Recommend credential review if entered.
Primary Sources: FTC Healthcare Scams; FBI Medicare Fraud; AARP Healthcare Fraud

Example Phrase: I downloaded an app that looked like Coinbase
Why It Matters: Fake crypto exchange apps mimic real brands and steal deposits.
Use With Context: Combine with money movement, app install timing, and inability to withdraw. Verify real exchange account separately.
Primary Sources: FBI Cybercrime; FTC Crypto Fraud; SEC Investment Fraud

Example Phrase: The website looked exactly like Kraken
Why It Matters: Lookalike exchange websites can show fake balances while diverting deposits.
Use With Context: Use with URL review, wallet addresses, and recent account setup. Capture site details for Fraud.
Primary Sources: FBI Cybercrime; FTC Crypto Fraud; SEC Investment Fraud

Example Phrase: Binance support told me to deposit more
Why It Matters: Fake support agents often request additional deposits to unlock fake profits.
Use With Context: Use with support chat screenshots, new wallet addresses, or withdrawal blocks.
Primary Sources: FBI Cybercrime; FTC Crypto Fraud; SEC Investment Fraud

Example Phrase: The wallet address was sent by the exchange agent
Why It Matters: A privately supplied wallet address may bypass legitimate exchange controls.
Use With Context: Use with crypto transfer requests, new payee, or remote coaching. Verify exchange instructions independently.
Primary Sources: FBI Cybercrime; FTC Crypto Fraud; SEC Investment Fraud

Example Phrase: The profits show in the app but I cannot withdraw
Why It Matters: Fake platforms commonly display false profits and block withdrawals until more money is sent.
Use With Context: Use with fee requests, tax payments, or romance/investment context. Escalate before more funds move.
Primary Sources: FBI Cybercrime; FTC Crypto Fraud; SEC Investment Fraud

Example Phrase: I need to pay a verification fee to withdraw crypto
Why It Matters: Withdrawal-fee traps are common in fake crypto platform fraud.
Use With Context: Use with fake profit screens and urgent requests. Warn customer legitimate platforms do not require outside fee transfers.
Primary Sources: FBI Cybercrime; FTC Crypto Fraud; SEC Investment Fraud

Example Phrase: The exchange emailed me a new wallet address
Why It Matters: Fake exchange emails can redirect customers to criminal wallet addresses.
Use With Context: Use with sender verification, recent email change, or phishing link review.
Primary Sources: FBI Cybercrime; FTC Crypto Fraud; SEC Investment Fraud

Example Phrase: The crypto platform asked for taxes before release
Why It Matters: Tax-before-release language is a common fake investment and crypto withdrawal scam.
Use With Context: Use with large displayed balances, withdrawal restrictions, or overseas platform references.
Primary Sources: FBI Cybercrime; FTC Crypto Fraud; SEC Investment Fraud

Example Phrase: A learning platform said to send funds to their wallet
Why It Matters: Fake crypto learning platforms can be onboarding paths into fraudulent wallets.
Use With Context: Use with online relationship, mentorship, or guaranteed-return language.
Primary Sources: FBI Cybercrime; FTC Crypto Fraud; SEC Investment Fraud

Example Phrase: The app shows guaranteed crypto returns
Why It Matters: Guaranteed returns are a core investment-fraud warning sign, especially in crypto.
Use With Context: Use with app screenshots, new deposits, and pressure to increase investment.
Primary Sources: FBI Cybercrime; FTC Crypto Fraud; SEC Investment Fraud

Example Phrase: Customer identity verified
Why It Matters: Documented identity verification lowers uncertainty after an alert.
Use With Context: Use to reduce score only after a real verification step, not as a generic note.
Primary Sources: Banking Best Practices; Fraud Prevention Guidelines

Example Phrase: Banker confirmed legitimate
Why It Matters: A banker confirmation can prevent unnecessary false-positive escalation.
Use With Context: Use when the banker independently confirmed the activity or source.
Primary Sources: Banking Best Practices; Fraud Prevention Guidelines

Example Phrase: I called the vendor directly
Why It Matters: Calling a known vendor number helps validate invoice or payment legitimacy.
Use With Context: Use to reduce risk when vendor contact information came from trusted records, not the suspicious message.
Primary Sources: Banking Best Practices; Fraud Prevention Guidelines

Example Phrase: Verified using known phone number
Why It Matters: Using a known phone number reduces risk of caller ID spoofing or imposter contact.
Use With Context: Use to lower score after documented out-of-band verification.
Primary Sources: Banking Best Practices; Fraud Prevention Guidelines

Example Phrase: Payee confirmed by prior record
Why It Matters: Known payee history can reduce risk when payment details match prior records.
Use With Context: Use with invoice/vendor payments where account details match bank history.
Primary Sources: Banking Best Practices; Fraud Prevention Guidelines

Example Phrase: Identity document reviewed
Why It Matters: Reviewing identity documents can reduce impersonation risk in branch interactions.
Use With Context: Use with customer-present situations and document the verification method.
Primary Sources: Banking Best Practices; Fraud Prevention Guidelines

Example Phrase: Transaction authorization confirmed
Why It Matters: Clear customer authorization can lower risk after suspicious activity review.
Use With Context: Use only when customer was contacted through safe channels and understood the transaction.
Primary Sources: Banking Best Practices; Fraud Prevention Guidelines

Example Phrase: Known trusted payee
Why It Matters: A trusted payee with history can reduce likelihood of new-payee fraud.
Use With Context: Use with payment history, normal amount, and no coercion indicators.
Primary Sources: Banking Best Practices; Fraud Prevention Guidelines

Example Phrase: Legitimate business confirmed
Why It Matters: Confirming the business is legitimate lowers risk in vendor or invoice scenarios.
Use With Context: Use with business records, known contacts, and matching payment instructions.
Primary Sources: Banking Best Practices; Fraud Prevention Guidelines

Example Phrase: Third party verification completed
Why It Matters: Independent third-party verification helps separate legitimate transactions from scams.
Use With Context: Use to reduce score after documented verification from a trusted source.
Primary Sources: Banking Best Practices; Fraud Prevention Guidelines
